title: Curvature-induced nonlinear anomalous Hall effect in thin magnetic shells
authors: Maria Teresa Mercaldo, Mario Cuoco, Carmine Ortix
abstract: Optoelectronic and nonlinear transport experiments probe the quantum geometric tensor of Bloch states, whose real and imaginary components -- the quantum metric and the Berry curvature -- are typically constrained by symmetry. Here, we show that geometric bending provides a route to engineer such responses in centrosymmetric ferromagnets. Curvature-induced strain gradients across the shell thickness break inversion symmetry and activate an orbital Rashba coupling. In the presence of in-plane magnetization and spin-orbit coupling, this generates spin textures with a nontrivial quantum geometry, leading to an intrinsic nonlinear anomalous Hall effect (NAHE) governed by the quantum metric and maximized when the magnetization aligns with the applied electric field. When geometric deformations further break twofold rotational symmetry around the out-of-plane axis, an additional NAHE emerges, maximal for magnetization perpendicular to the driving electric field and governed by the Berry curvature dipole, thus giving access to the imaginary component of the quantum geometric tensor. These results establish curved ferromagnetic shells as a platform for engineering anisotropic nonlinear transport and for selectively probing both components of the quantum geometric tensor.
summary: The paper demonstrates that the curvature of thin magnetic shells can be used to engineer nonlinear transport effects. By manipulating geometric bending, one can selectively probe both the quantum metric and the Berry curvature dipole, providing a platform for controlling the quantum geometry of Bloch states.
main_problem: How can geometric curvature and strain gradients in thin magnetic shells be used to engineer and probe the components of the quantum geometric tensor?
main_result: Geometric bending breaks inversion symmetry and activates orbital Rashba coupling, generating a nonlinear anomalous Hall effect (NAHE) governed by the quantum metric and the Berry curvature dipole.
method: Theoretical analysis of transport properties in curved ferromagnetic structures, focusing on the interplay between strain-induced symmetry breaking and spin-orbit coupling.
model_or_system: Thin ferromagnetic shells subject to geometric bending, which induces strain gradients and breaks inversion and rotational symmetries.
key_observables: Nonlinear anomalous Hall effect (NAHE), quantum metric, Berry curvature dipole, and spin textures.
important_parameters: In-plane magnetization direction, applied electric field orientation, and degree of geometric deformation/curvature.
main_assumptions: The system is treated within the framework of Bloch states and the quantum geometric tensor, assuming the presence of spin-orbit coupling and in-plane magnetization.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of probing the quantum geometric tensor, describes the mechanism of curvature-induced symmetry breaking and orbital Rashba coupling, analyzes the resulting NAHE components, and concludes with the implications for engineering anisotropic transport.
